questions.question: Explain the principle of Time Division Multiplexing (TDM) and calculate the number of time slots required to multiplex eight digital signals each operating at 64 kbps onto a link with a data rate of 512 kbps.
keyPoints: TDM interleaves samples from multiple channels in distinct time slots within a recurring frame.
Total bandwidth needed = sum of individual data rates: 8 × 64 kbps = 512 kbps.
Given the link capacity equals the total required bandwidth, one time slot per frame is needed for each of the eight signals (8 slots per frame).
If the link capacity were larger, additional idle slots could be inserted for synchronization.

questions.question: Compare Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM) and Wavelength Division Multiplexing (WDM) with respect to the medium used, the method of separating channels, and typical applications.
keyPoints: FDM divides the total bandwidth into distinct frequency bands for each channel; used in analog radio and television broadcasting.
WDM is essentially FDM applied to optical fibers, separating channels by wavelength (or colour) of light.
WDM offers much finer channel spacing (nanometre wavelengths) and higher aggregate data rates than FDM in electrical domains.
Practical applications: FDM in broadcast radio, TV, and microwave links; WDM in high‑capacity fiber‑optic backbone networks.

questions.question: Describe the concept of Code Division Multiplexing (CDM) used in CDMA. Explain how orthogonal codes enable multiple users to share the same frequency band simultaneously. Provide a simple numerical example with three users and four‑bit orthogonal (Walsh) codes, showing the transmitted composite signal and how a receiver recovers a specific user's data.
keyPoints: Each user is assigned a unique, orthogonal spreading code; all users transmit simultaneously over the same spectral band.
Orthogonal codes have zero cross‑correlation, so the inner product of any two distinct codes is zero.

Transmission: multiply user’s data bit (+1/‑1) by its code, sum the resulting chip sequences from all active users; the receiver recovers a user by taking the inner product of the composite signal with that user’s code.

Example: codes C1 = [1,1,1,1], C2 = [1,‑1,1,‑1], C3 = [1,1,‑1,‑1]; if user1 sends ‘1’, user2 sends ‘‑1’, user3 sends ‘0’, the composite chip vector = [3,1,1,‑1]; decoding with C1 yields (3·1 + 1·1 + 1·1 + ‑1·1)/4 = 1, confirming user1’s bit.

questions.question: A digital communication link uses synchronous TDM to combine four PCM voice signals, each sampled at 8 kHz with 8‑bit resolution. The link’s capacity is 2.048 Mbps. (i) Determine the frame structure: number of bits per frame, frame duration, and duration of one time slot. (ii) Explain how statistical multiplexing could be employed to improve link utilization and discuss the trade‑offs in terms of latency, overhead, and implementation complexity. (iii) Compare the advantages and limitations of synchronous TDM versus statistical multiplexing for voice traffic.
keyPoints: Each voice channel: 8 kHz × 8 bits = 64 kbps → 4 channels require 256 kbps. With a 2.048 Mbps link, each frame can contain 8 repetitions of the 4‑slot structure, giving 256 bits/frame; at 8 kHz frame rate, frame duration = 125 µs, slot duration = 125 µs/4 ≈ 31.25 µs.

Statistical multiplexing allocates time slots on‑demand, filling idle slots with active traffic, raising average throughput; however it introduces variable delay, requires buffering, and needs control bits for address/header, increasing overhead.
TDM provides deterministic latency and simple hardware but wastes bandwidth when some channels are idle; statistical multiplexing improves efficiency at the cost of added latency and complexity.
For voice, moderate, constant latency is acceptable; statistical multiplexing may cause jitter, which can be mitigated by jitter buffers, while TDM guarantees uniform timing.
